content.problemStatement:
Chef Chick loves to jump a lot. Once, it realised that it was on an infinitely long road, and decided to travel along this road by jumping.

Let's view the road as the xxx-axis in a 1D coordinate system. Initially, Chef Chick is at the coordinate x=0x=0x=0, and it wants to move only in the positive xxx-direction. Moreover, Chef Chick has NNN favourite integers a1,a2,…,aNa_1, a_2, \ldots, a_Na1​,a2​,…,aN​, and it wants to jump on the coordinates that are multiples of these favourite numbers — when its current position is xxx, it jumps to the smallest coordinate y>xy \gt xy>x such that yyy is an integer multiple of at least one of the values a1,a2,…,aNa_1, a_2, \ldots, a_Na1​,a2​,…,aN​; the length of such a jump is y−xy-xy−x.

This way, Chef Chick keeps jumping along the road forever in the positive xxx-direction. You need to find the length of the longest jump it will make, i.e. the largest integer ddd such that Chef Chick makes at least one jump with length ddd and never makes any jump with a greater length. It can be proved that such an integer always exists.
content.inputFormat:
First line contains an integer T. Each test case contains a single integer N, followed by N space-separated integers a1,a2,…,aNa_1, a_2, 
... a_Na1​,a2​,…,aN​.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, print a single line containing one integer — the length of the longest jump that Chick will make.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Example case 1: The sequence of coordinates on which Chef Chick would jump starts with (0,2,3,4,6,…)(0, 2, 3, 4, 6, \ldots)(0,2,3,4,6,…). A longest jump is e.g. from 444 to 666, with length 222.

Explanation:
Example case 2: The sequence of coordinates on which Chef Chick would jump starts with (0,2,3,4,5,6,…)(0, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, \ldots)(0,2,3,4,5,6,…). A longest jump is e.g. from 555 to 111111, with length 555555.

Explanation:
Example case 3: The sequence of coordinates on which Chef Chick would jump starts with (0,2,3,4,5,6,7,8,10,11,14,15,16,17,18,19,…)(0, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 10, 11, 14, 15, 16, 17, 18, 19, \ldots)(0,2,3,4,5,6,7,8,10,11,14,15,16,17,18,19,…). A longest jump is e.g. from 777 to 111111, with length 111111.

solution.approach: To solve this problem, we can use a simple brute force approach. We can iterate over all possible jump lengths and check if it's possible to make a jump of that length. If it is, we update the maximum jump length. We can use a set to store the multiples of each number to efficiently check if a number is a multiple of any of the given numbers.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T * N * max(a) * log(max(a))) where max(a) is the maximum value in the input
